content_markdown: A fixed mass of an ideal gas is heated at constant volume. Which **one** of the following graphs best shows the variation of Celsius temperature $t$ with pressure $p$ of the gas?

diagram_metadata.description: Four graphs showing pressure p on the y-axis and Celsius temperature t on the x-axis. Graph A shows a linear relationship with a positive y-intercept. Graph B shows a linear relationship passing through the origin. Graph C shows a linear relationship with a positive x-intercept. Graph D shows a curved exponential-like relationship with a positive y-intercept.

vibe_explanation: According to the ideal gas law $pV = nRT$, at constant volume, pressure is directly proportional to absolute temperature $T$ in Kelvin ($p \propto T$). Since $T = t + 273.15$, the relationship between pressure and Celsius temperature $t$ is $p = \frac{nR}{V}(t + 273.15)$. This is a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$ where the y-intercept $c$ is positive (representing the pressure at $0^\circ\text{C}$). Therefore, the graph should be a straight line that does not pass through the origin, but intersects the vertical axis at a positive value.
